sentences.text: Coverage Rationale Pre-test genetic counseling is strongly recommended in order to inform persons being tested about the advantages and limitations of the test as applied to a unique person.
sentences.label: CLAIM

sentences.text: Chromosome microarray testing using array comparative genomic hybridization (aCGH) and/or single-nucleotide polymorphism (SNP) array is proven and medically necessary for the following: Evaluation of an embryo/fetus in the following cases: o Intrauterine Fetal Demise or Stillbirth o Testing the products of conception following pregnancy loss o Individuals undergoing invasive prenatal testing (i.e., amniocentesis, chorionic villus sampling, or fetal tissue sampling) Chromosome Microarray Testing (Non-Oncology Conditions) Page 1 of 28 UnitedHealthcare Commercial and Individual Exchange Medical Policy Effective 01/01/2026 Proprietary Information of UnitedHealthcare.
sentences.label: CLAIM

sentences.text: Copyright 2026 United HealthCare Services, Inc. Evaluation of individuals with one or more of the following: o Autism spectrum disorder (ASD) o Isolated severe congenital heart disease o Multiple anomalies that are not specific to a Well-Delineated Genetic Syndrome and cannot be identified by a clinical evaluation alone o Developmental Delay/Intellectual Disability where a specific syndrome is not suspected Evaluation of biological parent or sibling of a fetus or child with an abnormal or equivocal finding on chromosome microarray testing results Chromosome microarray testing using aCGH or SNP array is unproven and not medically necessary for all other populations and conditions due to insufficient evidence of efficacy.
sentences.label: CLAIM

sentences.text: Note: Preimplantation genetic testing (PGT) is addressed in the Medical Policy titled Preimplantation Genetic Testing and Related Services.
sentences.label: CLAIM

sentences.text: Medical Records Documentation Used for Reviews Benefit coverage for health services is determined by the member specific benefit plan document and applicable laws that may require coverage for a specific service.
sentences.label: CLAIM

sentences.text: Medical records documentation may be required to assess whether the member meets the clinical criteria for coverage but does not guarantee coverage of the service requested; refer to the guidelines titled Medical Records Documentation Used for Reviews.
sentences.label: CLAIM

sentences.text: Definitions Developmental Delay: Developmental Delay may be used to describe children younger than 5 years of age who present with delays in the attainment of developmental milestones at the expected age (Moeschler and Shevell, 2014, reaffirmed 2019).
sentences.label: CLAIM

sentences.text: Intellectual Disability: A condition diagnosed before age 18 that includes below-average intellectual function and a lack of skills necessary for daily living (MedlinePlus, 2020).
sentences.label: CLAIM

sentences.text: Intrauterine Fetal Demise or Stillbirth: Fetal death at or after 20 weeks’ gestation [American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists (ACOG), Society of Maternal Fetal Medicine (SMFM), 2020, reaffirmed 2021].
sentences.label: CLAIM

sentences.text: Prenatal Diagnosis: A laboratory test performed on fetal deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA) or chromosomes before birth to determine if a fetus has a genetic or chromosomal disorder (ACOG, 2016a, reaffirmed 2023).
sentences.label: CLAIM

sentences.text: Well-Delineated Genetic Syndrome: A syndrome is a collection of recognizable traits or abnormalities that tend to occur together and are associated with a specific disease.
sentences.label: CLAIM

sentences.text: Distinguishing characteristics, such as specific facial features or other physical traits, lab tests, or family history can be used to identify a genetic syndrome. (
sentences.label: CLAIM

sentences.text: Examples of Well-Delineated Genetic Syndromes include but are not limited to: Down syndrome, Klinefelter syndrome, Marfan syndrome, neurofibromatosis type 1, osteogenesis imperfecta, Prader-Willi syndrome, Rett syndrome, trisomy 13 (Patau syndrome), Trisomy 18 (Edwards syndrome), Turner syndrome, and Williams syndrome.
sentences.label: CLAIM

heart and circulatory system Description of Services Genetic counseling is strongly recommended prior to chromosome microarray testing [also called chromosome microarray analysis (CMA)] in order to inform persons being tested about the advantages and limitations of the test as applied to their unique situation.
sentences.label: CLAIM

sentences.text: CMA includes array comparative genomic hybridization (aCGH) and/or single-nucleotide polymorphism (SNP) array (Levy and Wapner, 2018).
sentences.label: CLAIM

sentences.text: Chromosome Microarray Testing (Non-Oncology Conditions) Page 13 of 28 UnitedHealthcare Commercial and Individual Exchange Medical Policy Effective 01/01/2026 Proprietary Information of UnitedHealthcare.
sentences.label: CLAIM

sentences.text: Copyright 2026 United HealthCare Services, Inc. Chromosome abnormalities are a well-established cause of congenital anomalies, dysmorphic features, Developmental Delay (DD), Intellectual Disability (ID), and other neurodevelopmental disorders.
sentences.label: CLAIM

sentences.text: There are two types of CMA that are used for the detection of chromosomal abnormalities: aCGH and SNP array.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: These tests analyze multiple sequences of deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA) by identifying deletions and duplications across the genome simultaneously.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: The chromosomal microarray may be targeted in nature, assaying certain regions of the genome known to be associated with a specific syndrome or phenotype and/or may be genome-wide (Shaffer et al., 2007).
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: Currently, most clinical applications of CMA are being investigated for the diagnosis of chromosomal abnormalities in fetuses and newborns, and in children with developmental disorders.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: For diagnostic prenatal testing, CMA requires an invasive procedure (e.g., amniocentesis or chorionic villous sampling) to collect fetal cells.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: SNP array testing and aCGH are used for the detection of genomic copy number variations (CNVs).
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: CNVs are alterations that include deletion and/or duplication of one or more sections of DNA.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: This method allows the detection of chromosome imbalances that can provide more information than is detected by conventional chromosome analysis [e.g., standard karyotype or fluorescence in situ hybridization (FISH)].
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: The aCGH approach detects CNVs of a DNA sequence in an individual by comparing it to a control.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: The SNP array approach detects CNVs by using DNA probes that are specific to a single base pair site in the genome.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: The copy number is quantified by either hybridization of the individual’s DNA and control DNA (aCGH) or comparison of the individual’s DNA to a control reference DNA sequence (SNP array).
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: Areas of unequal hybridization (aCGH) or differences between an individual and reference DNA (SNP array) signify a DNA alteration, such as large deletions or duplications.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: CNVs may be benign, with no effect on clinical phenotype, or may be pathogenic and result in a variety of phenotypic abnormalities (Kearney et al., 2011).
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: If a CNV of unknown clinical significance is detected, a genomic database is used to determine if the abnormality has been previously reported and if it has been associated with a benign or proposed pathogenic condition.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: The disadvantages of CMA include the detection of variants of unknown clinical significance, false positive results that will require further testing, and the inability to detect certain chromosomal abnormalities such as balanced rearrangements where there is no net gain or loss of the chromosomal material (Fruhman and Van den Veyver, 2010; Bui et al., 2011).
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: Amniocentesis was performed on all participants and both chromosome karyotyping and CMA were completed for each individual.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: In the group of participants with FGR and structural malformations, the researchers found the detection rates of pathogenic copy number variations (pCNVs) to be significantly higher than those found in the group with FGR and abnormal ultrasound soft markers and those with isolated FGR. (
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: Based on these results, the authors propose that the chromosomal variations associated with FGR plus structural anomalies detected via ultrasound may pose significant risk, especially as the number of organ systems involved increases.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: They recommend standard genetic assessments in cases of FGR occurring after 32 weeks gestation as well as before and propose that submicroscopic imbalances at Xp22.33 and Xq28 may be related to the pathogenesis of FGR.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: The study was limited by its small sample size from only one facility; the authors encourage additional study of the value of CMA in cases of FGR with ultrasound soft marker abnormalities and continue to advocate for the use of CMA as a primary tool for prenatal diagnostics in older pregnant individuals and those with severe FGR despite the findings of this study. Nguyen et al. (
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: 2025) used single-nucleotide polymorphism (SNP)-based CMA to help identify the genetic variations present in fetuses found to have intrauterine growth restriction (IUGR) with malformations identified on ultrasound.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: This cross-sectional study enrolled 129 individuals aged 18 to 48 whose fetuses were diagnosed with IUGR.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: The majority of malformations were related to the cardiovascular system.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: Of the 75 cases where amniocentesis with karyotyping and SNP array testing were performed, 26 (35%) had genetic abnormalities identified.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: When comparing the findings of SNP array testing and karyotyping, SNP testing detected more than twice the number of total cases, identifying genetic anomalies that may have otherwise been missed by karyotyping.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: Although limited by its small size and observational design, this study’s outcomes led the authors to infer that CMA technology is more effective Chromosome Microarray Testing (Non-Oncology Conditions) Page 14 of 28 UnitedHealthcare Commercial and Individual Exchange Medical Policy Effective 01/01/2026 Proprietary Information of UnitedHealthcare.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: 2024) assessed the clinical utility of CMA performed in cases of pregnancies where the fetus was found to have congenital heart defects (CHD) identified through standard ultrasound anatomy scans, then confirmed via echocardiography.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: The prevalence of chromosomal abnormalities in these cases was also ascertained; both standard karyotyping and CMA were performed on all cases.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: The researchers determined that, in this study, the diagnostic yield of CMA for fetuses with CHD was 15.3% (98/642), which was substantially higher than the diagnostic yield of standard karyotyping at 8.3% (53/642).
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: Overall, the detection rate of clinically meaningful chromosome anomalies was significantly increased in the group with CHD plus extracardiac structural anomalies when compared with the isolated CHD group (33.1% vs 9.9%, p < 0.0001.)
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: Based on their findings, the authors recommend CMA as a first-tier test for fetuses found to have CHD, with special consideration given to cases of CHD with additional structural abnormalities or soft markers.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: The study was limited by the need for individuals to pay for invasive prenatal testing and the refusal of some participants to engage in further testing, leading to selection bias.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: In addition, the majority of participants declined follow-up whole exome sequencing (WES), which could have led to more complete genetic diagnoses.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: Another study assessing the use of SNP-based CMA to identify underlying genetic causes for fetal cardiac ultrasound abnormalities was undertaken by Ye et al. ( 2024).
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: This study included 2092 pregnant individuals who were divided into groups based on presence or absence of structural abnormalities identified via ultrasound as well as the specific type of abnormality found.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: above, the authors found that fetuses with non-isolated CHD had the highest frequency of overall chromosome abnormalities when compared to the isolated CHD group, the non-CHD group, and the control group (42.6% vs 14.1%, 14.9%, and 9.4% respectively).
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: They suggest that SNP-array based CMA is highly effective in boosting the diagnosis of abnormal copy number variations (CNVs) in fetuses with CHD but also emphasize the complexity of the etiologies of CHD, recommending further research that investigates the underlying mechanisms leading to CHD.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: To examine diagnostic yields of genomic disorders and syndromic pCNVs associated with pregnancy loss and to approximate the risk of spontaneous abortion (SAB) by comparing the incidence of genomic disorders and syndromic pCNVs between products of conception (POC) and infants, Peng et al. (
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: After further refinement of pCNV categories, diagnostic yield was found to be 0.8% (n = 296) for genomic disorders and syndromic pCNVs, accounting for approximately 31% of the pCNVs detected overall.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: Newborn incidence was estimated from population genetic studies and a case series of 32,587 children with DD where pCNVs were identified in 2312 individuals (Girirajan et al., 2012).
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: An additional five genomic disorders and two syndromic pCNVs were also found to have lower incidence in newborns than in POC, but the differences were statistically insignificant.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: The authors calculated the overall risk of SAB for major genomic disorders and syndromic pCNVs to be 38%; this is significantly lower than the overall risk of SAB for chromosomal abnormalities (94%).
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: They suggest that their results may assist with evidence-based interpretation of genomic disorders and syndromic pCNVs detected via CMA in prenatal diagnosis and provide better understanding of the relationship between genomic disorders and pCNVs and pregnancy loss.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: CMA provided informative results on 95% of cases compared to 67% with karyotyping, and CMA provided a 2% greater yield of pCNVs.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: The authors concluded that CMA resulted in diagnostic information in early pregnancy loss in significantly more cases when compared to conventional chromosome analysis.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: Clinical Practice Guidelines American College of Medical Genetics and Genomics (ACMG) The 2018 ACMG clinical practice report on genetic testing after CMA for the diagnosis of neurodevelopmental disability and congenital anomalies (Waggoner et al.)
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: states that “chromosomal microarray (CMA) is recommended as the first-tier test in evaluation of individuals with neurodevelopmental disability and congenital anomalies.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: CMA may not detect balanced cytogenomic abnormalities or uniparental disomy (UPD), and deletion/duplications and regions of homozygosity may require additional testing to clarify the mechanism and inform accurate counseling.” The ACMG (Cherry et al.,
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: 2017) published a practice resource guideline for laboratories addressing diagnostic testing following positive noninvasive prenatal screening (NIPS) which made the following recommendations: CMA on CVS or amniocentesis may be used for confirmatory diagnosis for abnormal NIPS results or as a reflex to normal karyotype analysis.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: CMA testing should be utilized for follow-up when small copy number changes are reported as positive on NIPS.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: Testing of POC and/or fetus by karyotype or CMA should be considered on a case basis when prenatal diagnosis is not possible.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: For neonates with abnormal physical findings which are not suggestive of the trisomy suggested by original screening, CMA is recommended.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: CMA is recommended when NIPS sex determination is not concordant with physical examination or other clinical evidence reveals possible disorder of sexual differentiation.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists (ACOG)/Society for Maternal Fetal Medicine (SMFM) In a 2020 (reaffirmed 2021) Obstetric Care Consensus, ACOG and SMFM address microarray analysis as it relates to the management of stillbirth.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: Microarray analysis is noted to be the preferred method for evaluating stillbirth as it not only detects aneuploidy but correspondingly detects CNVs that are not measurable by karyotype.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: Microarray analysis is also more likely to offer a genetic diagnosis due to its success with nonviable tissue, making it particularly valuable in analysis of stillbirths with congenital anomalies or when karyotype outcomes cannot be obtained.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: The consensus document concludes that incorporating microarray analysis into stillbirth work up results in improvements in test success rates and detection of genetic anomalies compared with conventional testing with karyotype.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: In a 2016 Committee Opinion on Microarrays and Next-Generation Sequencing Technology (ACOG, 2016a, reaffirmed 2023), ACOG and SMFM make the following recommendations for the use of chromosomal microarray analysis and newer genetic technologies in prenatal diagnosis: Most genetic changes identified by CMA that typically are not identified on standard karyotype are not associated with increasing maternal age; therefore, the use of this test can be considered for all women, regardless of age, who undergo prenatal diagnostic testing.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: Prenatal CMA is recommended for a patient with a fetus with one or more major structural abnormalities identified on ultrasonographic examination and who is undergoing invasive prenatal diagnosis.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: This test typically can replace the need for fetal karyotype.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: In a patient with a structurally normal fetus who is undergoing invasive prenatal diagnostic testing, either fetal karyotyping or a CMA can be performed.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: CMA of fetal tissue (i.e., amniotic fluid, placenta, or POC) is recommended in the evaluation of intrauterine fetal death or stillbirth when further cytogenetic analysis is desired because of the test’s increased likelihood of obtaining results and improved detection of causative abnormalities.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: Comprehensive patient pretest and posttest genetic counseling from an obstetrician–gynecologist or other health care provider with genetics expertise regarding the benefits, limitations, and results of chromosomal microarray analysis is essential.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: CMA should not be ordered without informed consent, which should include discussion of the potential to identify findings of uncertain significance, non-paternity, consanguinity, and adult-onset disease.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: CMA should be the primary test (replacing conventional karyotype) for patients undergoing prenatal diagnosis for the indication of a fetal structural abnormality detected by ultrasound (based on good/consistent scientific evidence: Level A).
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: Chromosomal microarray analysis may be used to confirm an abnormal FISH test (based on limited or inconsistent scientific evidence: Level B).
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: 2020): Diagnosis and management of FGR recommends: Pregnant women should be offered fetal diagnostic testing, including CMA, when FGR is detected and a fetal malformation, polyhydramnios, or both are present, regardless of gestational age.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: Pregnant women should be offered prenatal diagnosis testing with CMA when unexplained isolated FGR is diagnosed < 32 weeks of gestation.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: In an SMFM Consult Series publication (2016) on the use of CMA for prenatal diagnosis, SMFM makes the following recommendations: CMA should be offered when genetic analysis is performed in cases with fetal structural anomalies and/or stillbirth and replaces the need for fetal karyotype in these cases (GRADE 1A).
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: Providers should discuss the benefits and limitations of CMA and conventional karyotype with patients who are considering amniocentesis and chorionic villus sampling (CVS) and that both options be available to women who choose to undergo diagnostic testing (GRADE 1B).
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: The use of CMA is not recommended as a first-line test to evaluate first trimester pregnancy losses due to limited data (GRADE 1C).
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: Pre- and post-test counseling should be performed by trained genetic counselors, geneticists, or other providers with expertise in the complexities of interpreting CMA results (Best practice).
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: The SOGC indicates that genetic consultation and amniocentesis with CMA of fetal DNA and molecular analysis for congenital infections should be offered to pregnant individuals with suspected early-onset FGR, especially if there is evidence of structural abnormalities, polyhydramnios, or multiple soft markers, when there is no indication of a placental basis for FGR. (
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: Strong recommendation, high quality of evidence) Society of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists of Canada (SOGC)/Canadian College of Medical Geneticists (CCMG) A Joint Clinical Practice Guideline of the SOGC and CCMG recommended offering CMA in cases of multiple congenital anomalies revealed on ultrasound (II-1A) or fetal MRI.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: In addition, CMA was also recommended when single congenital defects in conjunction with other findings (e.g., IUGR, oligohydramnios) are detected.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: Prenatal CMA should be considered for certain malformations that have a high association with abnormal results.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: CMA is not recommended for pregnancies that are at low risk for a structural anomaly (Audibert et al., 2017).
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: An SOGC/CCMG Practice Guideline for the use of CMA for prenatal diagnosis and assessment of fetal loss in Canada (Armour et al.,
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: 2018) recommends the following: CMA may be offered following normal aneuploidy screen results when multiple fetal malformations are detected (II- 1A) or NT ≥ 3.5MM (II-2B).
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: Genetic counseling should be provided to obtain informed consent; parental decisions for reporting of incidental findings (II-2A); and for post-test results reporting counseling (III-A).
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: CMA resolution should be similar to postnatal CMA panels for the detection of small pathogenic variants.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: VUS smaller than 500 Kb deletion or 1 Mb duplication should not be reported in prenatal setting.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: VUS above such cut-offs should only be reported if there is significant evidence that deletion or duplication or the region may be pathogenic (III-B).
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: Secondary findings associated with significant childhood onset conditions should be reported; variants associated with adult-onset conditions should only be reported if previously requested by parents or if disclosure could prevent harm to family members (III-A).
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: Chromosome Microarray Testing (Non-Oncology Conditions) Page 19 of 28 UnitedHealthcare Commercial and Individual Exchange Medical Policy Effective 01/01/2026 Proprietary Information of UnitedHealthcare.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: The report addresses several types of genetic testing that may be used for the diagnosis of ASD including CMA, which is the most common first-tier test used to help diagnose ASD (Hyman et al., 2020).
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: In terms of clinical utility, Hayes found limited evidence from low-quality studies suggesting that genetic testing in individuals diagnosed with ASD may assist with medical management in some individuals, but no studies were found that included outcomes based on long-term follow-up or compared the outcomes of individuals who underwent genetic testing with a group of affected individuals who did not have the testing.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: Hayes concluded that the existing evidence is therefore insufficient to support clinical utility for use in those affected with ASD and their relatives (Hayes 2023; updated 2024). Landis et al. (
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: 2023) evaluated the association of CHD diagnoses with abnormal CMA findings in a study of 1363 children with CHD from nine pediatric cardiac centers in the U.S. Each participant had both CHD (per abnormal findings on echocardiogram) and at least one abnormal clinical finding on CMA.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: Cardiac phenotypes were described in detail and analytically grouped according to their relationship with abnormal CMA results.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: The authors concluded that the high percentage of pathogenic structural variations found via CMA in children with GDD/ID lends support to the use of CMA in individuals with a non-specific phenotype. Harris et al. (
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: 2020) reported on the diagnostic yield of genetic testing in toddlers with a Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders, Fifth Edition, diagnosis of ASD.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: The authors concluded that these results confirm the importance of genetic testing in toddlers diagnosed with ASD due to the 12% yield and lack of phenotypic differences between subjects with and without pathogenic findings.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: The authors reported that the diagnostic yields were significantly higher with co-existing CHDs (55%), facial dysmorphism (39%), microcephaly (34%), or hypotonia (35%).
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: Co-existing skeletal malformations (26%), brain malformations (24%) or epilepsy (24%) did not affect the diagnostic yield.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: Individual test type have specific benefits and limitations, and the utility of various tests may be different depending on individual circumstances.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: Decisions regarding testing may be influenced by factors including symptoms, turn-around time, insurance coverage, and cost.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: Due to the complex nature of genetic testing and potential implications it may have (e.g., impact on eligibility for life insurance, reproductive decisions, medical decisions), incorporating genetic counseling is encouraged.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: A clinical report addressing the identification of infants/children with DD (Lipkin & Macias, 2020) recommends that children with suspected GDD or ID have genetic testing including chromosomal microarray and fragile-X testing.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: In a 2014 clinical report, the Committee on Genetics for AAP stated that CMA is designated as a first-line test and replaces the standard karyotype and FISH subtelomere tests for the child with ID of unknown etiology.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: The authors recommend that CMA should be performed in all children with ID or GDDs (Moeschler and Shevell, 2014, reaffirmed 2019).
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: American Academy of Neurology (AAN) In a model coverage policy for chromosomal microarray analysis for ID, the AAN recommends the following inclusion criteria for microarray testing: In children with DD/ID or ASD according to accepted Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders-IV criteria.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: The AAN model coverage policy states that the following circumstances limit the value of microarray testing: Absence of an appropriate and informed consent from the patient, a parent (in case of minors) or a guardian (in persons with cognitive impairment) is necessary prior to testing.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: Inadequacy of knowledge about the test and the actions required to address the results of the test.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: Chromosomal microarray analysis would not be considered medically necessary when a diagnosis of a disorder or syndrome is readily apparent based on clinical evaluation alone.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: The AAN model coverage policy further indicates the presence of major and minor congenital malformations and dysmorphic features should be considered evidence that microarray testing will be more likely to yield a diagnosis.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: presented a diagnostic genetic testing algorithm for youth with developmental disorders including ASD, ID, or GDD, indicating that if there is a recognized genetic syndrome (e.g., Fragile X syndrome, PTEN hamartoma syndrome, Rett syndrome, tuberous Chromosome Microarray Testing (Non-Oncology Conditions) Page 22 of 28 UnitedHealthcare Commercial and Individual Exchange Medical Policy Effective 01/01/2026 Proprietary Information of UnitedHealthcare.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: If this testing does not yield a diagnosis, CMA and Fragile X testing are indicated.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: state that chromosomal microarray technologies are well accepted and used in evaluation of both constitutional and neoplastic disorders.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: For chromosomal imbalances related to multiple congenital anomalies, autism, and/or ID, CMA (including both array-CGH and SNP array) is considered the first-tier test.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: In the case of individuals where ultrasound has identified major fetal structural abnormalities and invasive prenatal diagnostic testing will be performed, CMA is the recommended test.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: This is also true for further workup of intrauterine fetal demise or stillbirth when parents/providers make the decision to pursue potential genetic diagnosis.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: The 2013 ACMG guideline for identifying the etiology of ASD lists chromosomal microarray testing (array-CGH or SNP array) as a first-tier diagnostic test for the evaluation of ASDs.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: According to the ACMG, many recognizable syndromes (i.e., Fragile X syndrome, Rett syndrome) have a firmly documented association with ASDs.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: The specific recommendations listed in the 2010 guideline are as follows (Manning and Hudgins, 2010, reaffirmed 2020): Cytogenetic microarray (CMA) testing for CNV is recommended as a first-line test in the initial postnatal evaluation of individuals with the following: o Multiple anomalies not specific to a well-delineated genetic syndrome. o Apparently non-syndromic DD/ID. o ASD.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: Further determination of the use of CMA testing for the evaluation of the child with growth retardation, speech delay, and other less-well studied indications is recommended, particularly via prospective studies and aftermarket analysis.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: These guidelines also do not specify what type of microarray platform should be used (i.e., microarray based CGH versus SNP microarray), although they do state that any ordering physician should be aware of the information generated and the limitations of the particular test performed.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: ACMG Practice Guidelines addressing the interpretation and reporting of microarray results in postnatal clinical settings were published in 2011 and include recommendations regarding how to define the various types of CNVs (pathogenic versus benign versus uncertain significance), the confirmation of abnormal results, the information that should be included in laboratory reports, and how to handle unanticipated or ambiguous results.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: Of importance, it is noted that if a CNV is identified that has unknown clinical significance, the parents of the proband should be tested to determine if the CNV is de novo or inherited, which may allow the clinician to determine the clinical significance of the result (Kearney et al., 2011).
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: Fragile X testing is advised when family history or clinical symptoms are suggestive of this disorder.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: Further recommendations include the use of WES or comprehensive gene panels as second-tier testing for individuals with GDD/ID.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: The CCMG does not recommend genetic tests for individuals with neurodevelopmental disorders when GDD, ID, or ASD is not present unless the phenotype is suggestive of a syndromic etiology or an inherited metabolic disease (Carter et al., 2023).
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: International Standards for Cytogenomic Array (ISCA) Consortium The ISCA reviewed the literature and meta-analyses on the clinical indications and diagnostic utility of chromosomal microarray testing and issued a consensus statement recommending that CMA be the first-tier clinical diagnostic test for individuals with developmental disabilities or congenital anomalies, followed by specific gene testing for the suspected condition(s) if CMA results are negative (Miller et al., 2010).
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: Chromosome Microarray Testing (Non-Oncology Conditions) Page 23 of 28 UnitedHealthcare Commercial and Individual Exchange Medical Policy Effective 01/01/2026 Proprietary Information of UnitedHealthcare.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: Genetic tests are regulated under the Clinical Laboratory Improvement Amendments (CLIA) of 1988.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: Accessed February 20, 2025) Refer to the following website for a list of nucleic acid-based tests/platforms that have been cleared or approved by the FDA’s Center for Devices and Radiological Heath: https://www.fda.gov/medical-devices/in-vitro-diagnostics/nucleic-acid- based-tests. sentences.label: PREMISE
